title: Untitled
research_problem: Developing a pedestrian microsimulation model for Chinese cities that accurately represents pedestrian signal non-compliance (red light running) behavior, which differs significantly from pedestrian behavior in developed countries due to high violation rates and mixed traffic characteristics
method_summary: A pedestrian microsimulation model that classifies pedestrians into two types (law-obeying and opportunistic) and simulates their decision-making at signalized intersections. The model incorporates external factors including policeman presence, other pedestrians' behavior, and vehicle gaps. Parameters are calibrated using questionnaires (495 respondents in Xi'an) and videotape data (989 samples extracted from a zebra crossing). Gap acceptance criterion ΔT is calculated based on pedestrian estimation of vehicle arrival time.
tasks: modeling pedestrian red light running behavior; simulating pedestrian gap acceptance at signalized intersections; representing mixed traffic characteristics in Chinese cities
datasets: Questionnaire data from 495 respondents in Xi'an (November 22-23, 2003); Videotape data from zebra crossing outside Southeast Gate of Xi'an Jiaotong University (989 samples)
metrics: proportion of red light jumpers; proportion of violation followers; pedestrian arriving interval distribution; gap acceptance (ΔT) distribution
claim_text: The model successfully represents the high rate of pedestrian red light running in Chinese cities and the mixed characteristics of traffic flows
limitations: Current model only models pedestrians' decision making according to instantaneous states of oncoming vehicles; does not account for vehicle acceleration/deceleration changes | Calculation method of ΔT needs improvement for turning vehicles to make model more practical | Questionnaire respondents may not represent all pedestrians; some answers may not reflect actual daily behavior | Model calibrated specifically for Xi'an may have limited generalizability to other Chinese cities with different traffic patterns
notes: The paper addresses a significant gap in traffic microsimulation for developing countries, specifically China. Key contribution is the behavioral model incorporating pedestrian types and external factors. The 2003 Guangzhou report cited shows 23.3% average illegal crossing rate, reaching 66.7% in certain sections, justifying the need for this model. Police presence was found to have minimal impact on pedestrian compliance at the time of study.

title: Untitled
research_problem: Limited pedestrian behavior models exist for signalized crosswalks, where pedestrians exhibit group/individual evasion with surrounding pedestrians, collision avoidance with conflicting vehicles, and responses to signal control and crosswalk boundary.
method_summary: A modified social force model is developed for pedestrian behavior at signalized crosswalks, incorporating five force types: driving force toward destination, repulsive/attractive force from crosswalk boundary, repulsive force from surrounding pedestrians (group and individual evasive behavior), repulsive force from conflicting left-turning vehicles, and attractive force from signal phase. The model uses Weibull distribution for desired direction, binary Logit for stop/go decisions at pedestrian flashing green phase, and elliptical force fields for collision avoidance. Parameters are calibrated by separating measurable parameters (directly estimated from trajectory data) from non-measurable parameters (derived by Maximum Likelihood Estimation).
datasets: Kanayama intersection north crosswalk in Nagoya City, Japan - 1352 pedestrians and 124 left-turning vehicles; 97,200 position samples from video extraction every 0.5s
metrics: Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) for position: 5.7%; Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) for speed: 10.5%; Passing position distribution at three cross-sections (t-test); Lane-formation number comparison; Passing time distribution
claim_text: The modified social force model achieves accurate pedestrian trajectory estimation with MAPE of 5.7% for position and 10.5% for speed
limitations: Pedestrian reaction to vehicles is more sensitive in simulation than in real world - peak shift in estimated data (2.5m) is larger than observed (2m) | Model does not account for pedestrian heterogeneous characteristics such as age, gender, and partner relationship | The lane-formation number in simulation (3.5) differs from observed (2.8), indicating some deviation from real-world behavior
notes: Paper published in Transportation Research Part C: Emerging Technologies, Volume 40, March 2014; Study site: Kanayama intersection in Nagoya City, Japan (left-hand traffic); Model considers special signal phase: Pedestrian Flashing Green (PFG); All non-measurable parameters are statistically significant at 95% confidence level (p < 0.05); Two-stage calibration approach separating measurable and non-measurable parameters is proposed as a contribution

title: Untitled
research_problem: Modeling pedestrian road crossing behavior in traffic system micro-simulation for Chinese cities, specifically addressing the high rate of pedestrian signal non-compliance (red light running) which differs significantly from behavior in developed countries
method_summary: A pedestrian behavioral model classifying pedestrians into two types (law-obeying and opportunistic) with parameters calibrated via questionnaires and videotape data. Uses gap acceptance criterion (DT) representing time difference between vehicle arrival and pedestrian crossing time. Includes effects of policeman presence, other pedestrians' behavior, and vehicle flow on pedestrian decisions.
datasets: Questionnaire data from 495 respondents in Xi'an (200 students + 300 pedestrians); Videotape data from zebra crossing outside Southeast Gate of Xi'an Jiaotong University (989 samples of DT)
metrics: proportion_of_red_light_jumpers; proportion_of_violation_followers; DT (time gap acceptance criterion)
claim_text: The proposed model successfully represents the high rate of pedestrian red light running and mixed traffic characteristics in Chinese cities
limitations: Model only considers pedestrian decision making based on instantaneous states of oncoming vehicles, not accounting for vehicle acceleration/deceleration | DT calculation method needs improvement for turning vehicles to make model more practical | Questionnaire respondents may not represent all pedestrians; self-reported behavior may not reflect actual behavior | Model calibrated for Xi'an may not generalize to other Chinese cities with different traffic conditions
notes: Published in Transportation Research Part A, Volume 40, 2006, Pages 280-290; Research conducted at School of Electrical Engineering, Xi'an Jiaotong University, China; Model includes three key parameters: r1 (proportion of law-obeying pedestrians), r2 (proportion of Type I who follow violators), r3 (proportion accepting a certain DT gap); Adjusted r2 from 0.69 to 0.35 based on analysis of deviation between simulation and field data; Pedestrian walking speed assumed as 1.2 m/s in calculations

title: Untitled
research_problem: Identify potential external features on fully autonomous vehicles (FAVs) that help pedestrians understand the vehicle's intended behavior at crosswalks, improve receptivity toward FAVs, and influence crossing behavior
method_summary: VR-based experimental study with 30 participants using a pedestrian simulator (Unity 5, HTC Vive). 4x4 randomized complete block design testing 4 visual features (walking silhouette, 'braking' text, smiling curve, no visual) and 4 audible features (horn, music, verbal message, no audio). Collected both objective measures (crossing time, waiting time from headset tracking) and subjective measures (feature ratings, receptivity surveys, pedestrian behavior questionnaire)
datasets: Validated pedestrian VR simulator (Deb, Carruth, et al., 2017); Pedestrian Behavior Questionnaire (PBQ) - 20 items; Pedestrian Receptivity Questionnaire for FAVs (PRQF); Personal Innovativeness Scale (4 items, adapted from Agarwal & Prasad, 1998); Simulation Sickness Questionnaire (SSQ)
metrics: feature_rating_7point_scale; crossing_time_seconds; waiting_time_seconds; receptivity_score; personal_innovativeness_score
claim_text: Pedestrian receptivity toward FAVs significantly increased with the inclusion of external features
limitations: Single controlled scenario with FAV approaching from one direction only and no other traffic in the virtual environment | Proposed features indicated safe crossing conditions only - vehicle stopped at every trial; did not test feature termination or mixed signals | First exposure to FAV for all participants; results may differ with repeated exposure or real-world experience | Simple intersection design without signals, vehicle operators, or environmental sounds may not reflect complex real-world traffic
notes: Study used validated VR platform confirmed to produce similar pedestrian behavior to real traffic environments. Average walking speed in simulator (1.54 m/sec) was faster than general walking speed (1.20 m/sec), suggesting uncertainty when crossing in front of driverless vehicles. Personal innovativeness significantly influenced waiting time (higher innovativeness = shorter wait).

title: Untitled
research_problem: Examined pedestrians' self-reported violating crossing behavior intentions by applying and extending the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB). The study added new factors including descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency to evaluate their respective impacts on pedestrians' behavior intentions.
method_summary: Survey-based study using questionnaires with scenario-based items about pedestrian red light violations at intersections. Data collected from 260 respondents in Dalian, China, and analyzed using Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) with maximum likelihood estimation.
datasets: 260 completed and valid questionnaires from pedestrian survey in Dalian, China
metrics: CMIN/DF_ratio; RMSEA; P-close; CFI; regression_weights; Cronbach_alpha
claim_text: Instrumental attitude was a significant predictor of pedestrian violating behavior intention across all four models tested
limitations: Self-reported behavior may introduce bias, especially for negative behaviors | Relatively small number of items included in each construct to maintain internal reliability | Perceived behavioral control was not significant, possibly due to the scenario not being sufficiently risky or challenging | Perceived risk was not significant, suggesting pedestrians may not adequately perceive susceptibility and seriousness of risk
notes: Study extends TPB by adding descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency. Descriptive norm captured pedestrians' perception of whether family and friends perform the behavior. Conformity tendency measured tendency to modify behavior when around others. Model 4 with three predictors (instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, conformity tendency) provided best predictive utility.

title: Untitled
research_problem: Investigating how mobile phone distractions (music distraction, phone conversation distraction, and text distraction) affect pedestrian crossing behavior and visual attention allocation at a signalized intersection in an outdoor environment
method_summary: Outdoor-environment experiment with 28 college student pedestrians conducted at a signalized intersection. Two HD video cameras recorded crossing behavior, and Tobii Pro Glasses 2 eye tracker recorded visual attention. Three crossing behavior indicators (initiate time, look left and right, crossing speed) and five eye movement indicators (pupil diameter, scanning frequency, fixation points, fixation time, fixation duration) were measured across four conditions: undistracted (S0), music distraction (S1), phone conversation distraction (S2), and text distraction (S3). Repeated-measure ANOVA with Bonferroni-corrected post-hoc comparisons was used for analysis.
metrics: initiate_time; look_left_and_right_frequency; crossing_speed; pupil_diameter; scanning_frequency; fixation_points; fixation_time; fixation_duration
claim_text: Text distraction causes the greatest impairment to pedestrian crossing performance, followed by phone conversation distraction and music distraction, based on significant differences across all three crossing behavior variables and five visual attention variables
limitations: Small sample size (n=28) limited to college students may restrict generalizability to overall pedestrian population | Participants were restricted from self-regulation and behavioral adaptation, so it is unclear whether they would use mobile phones the same way during daily life | Data collection was limited to one specific signalized intersection with very low traffic volume (<5 vehicles per minute), which may not represent more complex traffic environments | Experimental design did not exactly conform to participants daily life conditions, including use of unfamiliar phone and selection of mental arithmetic tasks for conversation and texting | Study only examined three specific mobile phone tasks (music, phone conversation, text), excluding other common tasks such as mobile internet browsing
notes: Text distraction had the highest impairment effect on crossing performance, followed by phone conversation and music; Music distraction generated cognitive load (increased pupil diameter) but less behavioral impact than other distractions; Pedestrians distracted by text looked left and right 44% fewer times than undistracted pedestrians (2.50 vs 4.46 times); Crossing speed was significantly slower for phone conversation (p=0.015) and text (p=0.032) compared to undistracted condition; The study used Areas of Interest (AOIs) analysis: phone area, crosswalk area, traffic signal area, right traffic area, and left traffic area

title: Untitled
research_problem: Identify factors affecting pedestrian red light running violation propensity at signalized urban intersections in Hong Kong, including personal factors (demographics, behavior) and environmental factors (signal time, traffic condition, presence of other pedestrians)
method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong. Crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians during red time of 652 cycles were captured. A random parameter logit model was developed to measure associations between red light running propensity and explanatory factors, accounting for unobserved heterogeneity. Both individual-level (personal demographics, behavior) and cycle-level (traffic condition, signal time) factors were incorporated, along with interaction effects.
tasks: Examine effects of pedestrian demographics (gender, age) on red light running propensity; Analyze influence of social factors (presence of companion, number of pedestrians around, violation by other pedestrians); Evaluate effects of signal time attributes (time to green, red time); Assess traffic condition effects (traffic volume, percentage of heavy vehicles); Investigate interaction effects between personal and environmental factors
datasets: Video observation data from 6 signalized crosswalks in Hong Kong (hot spots of pedestrian crashes); 6320 pedestrians observed during red signal phase; 652 traffic cycles recorded
metrics: Binary outcome (red light running vs. no violation); Odds ratios; Marginal effects and elasticities; AIC for model comparison
claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity to run red lights compared to males (coefficient = -0.405, odds ratio = 0.667), but this association is moderated by traffic volume and presence of other violators
limitations: Study focuses on propensity of red light running but does not assess safety outcomes (i.e., associated crash risk) | Effect of vehicular speed on pedestrian red light running behavior was not measured, though it is correlated with crash risk and severity | Observation survey lacks data on pedestrian safety perception, attitude, and individual risk assessment which could explain violation decisions | Study was conducted at specific urban locations in Hong Kong; findings may not generalize to other contexts with different traffic signal systems (e.g., locations with pedestrian countdown displays)

title: Untitled
research_problem: Understanding pedestrian street-crossing behavior at signalized intersections by modeling pedestrians' intended waiting times during the red-man phase of traffic lights. The research addresses the gap that while pedestrian behavior has received increasing attention, very little methodological development exists for pedestrian waiting time models at signalized intersections.
method_summary: The paper develops a statistical model for pedestrian intended waiting times by: (1) starting with a bounded Pareto distribution for individual pedestrians under specific traffic conditions; (2) incorporating vehicle time headway using Cowan's model with tracking and free components; (3) using statistical unconditioning to integrate over the distribution of headways; (4) deriving a mixture distribution with four components that characterizes the interaction between pedestrians and vehicles. The model produces a U-shaped distribution showing that many pedestrians cross immediately while many wait for the entire red-man phase. Parameter estimation uses the EM algorithm with BIC for model selection.
method_family: Statistical mixture model / Bounded waiting time distribution
tasks: Pedestrian intended waiting time modeling; Risk analysis of pedestrian street crossings
datasets: Field data from signalized intersection in Kunming, China (283 valid observations, red-man phase duration 75s, total cycle 110s)
metrics: Chi-square goodness-of-fit test; BIC (Bayesian Information Criterion) for model selection
claim_text: The proposed model produces a U-shaped distribution for pedestrian intended waiting times at signalized intersections, characterizing the nature that there are large proportions of pedestrians who cross immediately and those who wait for the entire red-man phase
limitations: The model is based on data from a single intersection in Kunming, China, limiting generalizability to other locations | Data collection occurred only on weekend days during specific time periods (10am-12pm and 2pm-4pm), potentially missing weekday rush-hour behavior | The analysis uses univariate approach focusing only on waiting time, without considering multiple risk factors simultaneously | The model requires censored data handling due to observations interrupted by green-man signal
notes: The paper was published in Transportation Research Part B in 2013; The author is Baibing Li from Loughborough University; The bounded Pareto distribution is a transformation of the Pareto distribution to model waiting times with upper bound C; The model classifies pedestrians into risk-taking and risk-averse categories; EM algorithm with ECM (Expectation Conditional Maximization) variant is used for parameter estimation

title: Untitled
research_problem: The interaction between vehicles and pedestrians at unsignalized midblock crosswalks is not well understood. Existing microscopic traffic flow models (car-following models) do not consider the interaction between vehicle yielding (VY) and pedestrian gap acceptance (PGA) behaviors, making it difficult to analyze the impact on traffic flow.
method_summary: A microscopic traffic flow model was developed by extending the Full Velocity Difference (FVD) car-following model to incorporate vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance behaviors. The model uses logit models for both VY and PGA decision-making, integrated into the car-following framework. Simulation is conducted using time-step simulation in MATLAB.
method_family: Microscopic traffic flow model (car-following model) with probabilistic behavioral models (logit)
tasks: Modeling vehicle-pedestrian interaction at unsignalized midblock crosswalks, analyzing traffic flow effects, evaluating operational performance
datasets: Empirical data from two unsignalized midblock crosswalks in Shanghai, China: 295 Zhenhua Rd. (313 veh/h/ln, 251 ped/h) and 1565 Lingshi Rd. (406 veh/h/ln, 172 ped/h)
metrics: Average vehicular delay (seconds); Average pedestrian waiting time (seconds); Relative error between model and survey results; Level of Service (LOS) thresholds
claim_text: The proposed model can accurately describe vehicle-pedestrian interaction at unsignalized midblock crosswalks with maximum relative error less than 10%
limitations: The model does not consider illegal pedestrian crossing behavior (crossing at locations without crosswalks), which is common in developing countries | The model does not account for the combined effect of adjacent intersections on vehicle arrival distributions | The study parameters were calibrated using data from Shanghai, China; generalizability to other contexts may be limited
notes: The study provides practical recommendations for transportation planners: unsignalized midblock crosswalks are suitable for roads with 4 or fewer lanes; signal control should be considered when pedestrian or vehicular volumes exceed certain thresholds. The model uses logit models for behavioral decisions and achieves acceptable accuracy with less than 10% relative error.

title: Untitled
research_problem: Understanding pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections under mixed traffic conditions, including crossing speed variations, compliance with traffic signals, and pedestrian-vehicular interactions in highly populous cities
method_summary: Data collected from 775 pedestrian samples at 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai using video recording during peak hours. Statistical tests including Pearson's correlation coefficient, ANOVA, and Student t-test performed at 95% confidence interval using SPSS 16.0. Logistic regression models developed to model odds of pedestrian noncompliance and pedestrian-vehicular interaction.
method_family: Statistical analysis (ANOVA, Pearson correlation, t-test) + Logistic regression modeling
tasks: Analyze pedestrian crossing speed variations; Identify factors affecting pedestrian compliance with traffic signals; Analyze pedestrian-vehicular interactions; Develop logistic regression models for noncompliance and interaction prediction
datasets: 775 pedestrian samples from 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai: Swami Vivekanand Junction (166 samples), Mahim Junction (272 samples), Holkar Junction (337 samples)
metrics: Crossing speed (mean, SD, 15th/50th/85th percentiles); Crossing Speed Deviation Factor (CSDF); Pedestrian compliance rate; Odds Ratio (OR) from logistic regression; Pearson correlation coefficients; F-values from ANOVA
claim_text: Pedestrian crossing speed at signalized intersections varies significantly from the IRC standard of 1.2 m/s, with observed mean crossing speed of 1.34 m/s
limitations: Sample limited to 775 pedestrians from 3 intersections in single city (Mumbai); may not generalize to other contexts | Study conducted only during peak hours; non-peak hour behavior not captured | Crosswalk length varies between sites (13.5-31.5m) which may affect crossing speed calculations | Fixed signal cycle times at selected sites; results may differ for adaptive signal systems
notes: Study introduces Crossing Speed Deviation Factor (CSDF) as new metric for understanding speed variations; CSDF shows strong negative correlation (R²=0.926) with pedestrian volume; Female pedestrians have lower odds of noncompliance (OR=0.569) and interaction (OR=0.533) compared to males; Adult pedestrians have much higher odds (OR=8.074) of interaction compared to children; Only 44% overall compliance rate found, with significant variation across locations (34%-64%)

title: Untitled
research_problem: Developing a probabilistic model to assess pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections for connected vehicles and active safety systems, specifically addressing the hazardous collision scenario between pedestrians and turning vehicles
method_summary: A Dynamic Bayesian Network (DBN) model that integrates intersection context information (traffic signal phases) with pedestrian behavior (crossing decision, motion type, position). The model uses particle filtering for state estimation including position, crossing decision (cross/wait), and motion type (standing/walking/running). The model incorporates logistic regression for decision-making and motion transitions, gamma distributions for speed modeling, and constant-speed/direction dynamics.
datasets: Real traffic data collected at a signalized intersection in Japan (289 pedestrian sequences: 251 cross-pedestrians, 38 wait-pedestrians); Data collected from vehicle-mounted cameras (3 monocular cameras, 13 fps, 1024x768 resolution); Pedestrian grounding points and motion types manually labeled; Trajectories projected to 2D ground plane
metrics: decision_recognition_accuracy_precision; motion_recognition_confusion_matrices; positioning_error_mean_standard_deviation; recognition_rates_at_different_time_from_decision
claim_text: The proposed DBN+PF system correctly recognizes pedestrian crossing decisions with accuracy higher than 86% at various noise levels (rn=0.1, 0.4, 1.0m)
limitations: Running motion is difficult to distinguish from walking motion due to overlapping speed distributions (recognition rate 35-56%) | Larger observation noises cause recognition delay and decreased accuracy for decision and motion recognition | The model assumes pedestrians make decisions at the onset of PFG, which may not always hold in reality | Model trained on data from Japan (left-hand traffic, specific signal phases PG/PFG/PR) - may not generalize to other regions | Path prediction is limited to straight trajectories; curved trajectories cause positioning errors due to constant-direction model assumption | Limited wait-pedestrian data (38 vs 251 cross-pedestrians) due to data collection constraints
notes: Paper belongs to Virtual Special Issue on Connected Autonomous Vehicles. The three-phase pedestrian signal system in Japan (PG/PFG/PR) is similar to systems in other Asian countries. The system targets near-side pedestrians (left side to left-turning vehicle in left-hand traffic). Computational cost is 5.1s per 1.0s data (13 frames) with 2000 particles using single-thread implementation.

title: Untitled
research_problem: Pedestrian safety at signalized intersections is compromised by sudden, unexpected speed changes that drivers cannot anticipate, leading to hazardous pedestrian-vehicle conflicts. The study aims to quantitatively model sudden speed changes of pedestrians as they cross signalized crosswalks under uncongested conditions.
method_summary: Discrete choice model (multinomial logit) to determine acceleration/deceleration event occurrence based on necessary walking speed to complete crossing (Vnec-Vcur), current speed, and presence of turning vehicles in conflict area. Linear regression for acceleration magnitude, normal distribution regression for deceleration magnitude. Monte Carlo simulation generates full pedestrian speed profiles. Video image processing (TrafficAnalyzer) extracts pedestrian trajectories at 0.5s intervals.
datasets: Video data from 5 crosswalks at 3 intersections in Nagoya City, Japan (Kanayama, Ueda, Fushimi); 4009 aggregated data samples (5s intervals); Pedestrian trajectory data: position every 0.5s via manual tracking
metrics: modified_rho_squared; crossing_time_distribution; vehicle_yield_rate; post_encroachment_time_pet
claim_text: The proposed model represents pedestrian travel time distribution more accurately than the constant speed model
limitations: The model underestimates acceleration events with 43.8% error in predicting actual acceleration events | Pedestrian-pedestrian interaction is not considered because study sites had low pedestrian traffic volumes | Pedestrian characteristics such as age, gender, and distraction (smartphone use) were not considered despite their potential impact on speed change behavior | Manual tracking using video image processing may introduce measurement errors (0.14m to 0.38m) | Limited sample size from only 5 sites prevented capturing significant impact of some variables like PFG/PG length, crosswalk length, and elapsed time from PFG onset | The conflict area dummy variable did not work significantly in model estimation; more precise approaching vehicle information may improve accuracy
notes: Study uses data from Iryo-Asano et al. (2015). Model integrated with vehicle maneuver models (Alhajyaseen et al. 2012a, 2013a) for safety assessment simulation. Sites selected to minimize pedestrian interactions under low-to-medium demand. No countdown signals at observation sites. Turn on red prohibited at all sites.
